I have listened to English podcasts for years.
I started English at school back in the 1980s.
I have never given up on languages.
I learned French when I worked in France.
You have been quiet, just watching the room. I am Astrid, from Bergen. Mind if I join you?
Please do! I am Marcelo, from Brazil. I have been traveling for three weeks, and I am just enjoying the sound of all these languages.
Brazil! I have been to São Paulo once, years ago. So, how many languages do you speak?
Portuguese, of course, and French. I picked it up back in my thirties, when I worked for a French company for eighteen years.
Impressive. And your English is great too. Did you study it for long?
On and off for forty years! I started at school, I gave it up many times, but I have never really stopped. I keep coming back to it.
Well, it clearly worked. You have settled into the conversation like a local. I grew up with Norwegian and English, but I gave up on Spanish after a year.
Ha! That is the goal, to fit in and talk to everyone. Over the years, I have learned that you do not study a language, you live it.
I picked up French by living it, not by studying it.
I started English back in the 1980s, and I never gave up.
I have come back to English again and again.
You don't study a language. You live it.

Hi Marcelo, it's Astrid, from the bar in Reykjavik. I really enjoyed our conversation the other night. I don't often meet someone who has traveled as much as you have. I've been thinking about what you said, that you don't study a language, you live it. It really stuck with me. A group of us are meeting in Bergen next month to practice our English over dinner, and I would love for you to join, even by video call. You picked up French by living it, so you'll fit right in. Let me know. Take care, and keep cycling.

Welcome back to the show. Today, a question I get all the time: what is the best way to learn a language as an adult? Here is the honest answer. The people who succeed are almost never the ones with the best memory. They are the ones who stick with it. They make mistakes, they feel silly, and they keep going anyway. Research shows that adults who immerse themselves in real conversation, even for twenty minutes a day, improve far faster than those who only study grammar rules. So my advice is simple: stop trying to be perfect, and start trying to communicate. Find real people, have real conversations, and let the language grow. That is how fluency really happens.

I want to fit in and talk to everyone I meet.
